title: Web Fundamentals: HTML, CSS & JavaScript
description: Build real web pages from scratch. Learn the three pillars of the web — HTML for structure, CSS for style, and JavaScript for interactivity. Every lesson produces something you can see in a browser, because code should serve people, not just pass tests.

lessons.title: Building a Page Structure
objectives: Understand the purpose of HTML as the skeleton of every web page
Use semantic tags (html, head, body, header, main, footer) to organize content
Create a valid HTML5 document from scratch
hints: Every HTML page starts with <!DOCTYPE html> — this tells the browser which version of HTML you're using
The <head> contains metadata (title, charset) while <body> contains everything the user sees
Semantic tags like <header>, <main>, and <footer> tell browsers and screen readers what each section means
assessmentCriteria: Page has a valid DOCTYPE declaration
Document contains html, head, and body elements
Page uses semantic elements: header, main, and footer
Head includes a title and charset meta tag

lessons.title: Headings, Paragraphs & Lists
objectives: Use heading tags (h1-h6) to create a content hierarchy
Write paragraphs and apply emphasis with <strong> and <em>
Build ordered and unordered lists to organize information
hints: Headings go from h1 (most important) to h6 (least) — use them in order, don't skip levels
<strong> means strong importance (usually bold), <em> means emphasis (usually italic)
<ul> creates bullet lists, <ol> creates numbered lists — both use <li> for items
assessmentCriteria: Page uses h1 through at least h3 in proper hierarchy
Content includes paragraphs with inline emphasis elements
Page contains both an ordered and unordered list
Lists have at least 3 items each

lessons.title: Colors, Fonts & Visual Identity
objectives: Apply colors using named colors, hex codes, and RGB values
Set font families, sizes, and weights to establish visual hierarchy
Understand the cascade — how CSS rules are applied and overridden
hints: Colors can be named ('crimson'), hex (#DC143C), or rgb(220, 20, 60) — they all work
Use font-family with fallbacks: font-family: 'Georgia', 'Times New Roman', serif
More specific selectors override less specific ones — an ID beats a class beats an element
assessmentCriteria: Stylesheet defines a body background color and text color
At least two different font families are used
Colors are applied using at least two different formats (hex, rgb, named)
Headings have distinct styling from body text

lessons.title: Layout with Flexbox
objectives: Use display: flex to create flexible, responsive layouts
Control alignment and spacing with justify-content and align-items
Build a card-based layout that adapts to different screen widths
hints: display: flex on a container makes its children flex items — they'll line up in a row by default
flex-wrap: wrap lets items flow to the next line instead of shrinking to fit
justify-content controls horizontal spacing, align-items controls vertical alignment
assessmentCriteria: At least one container uses display: flex
Layout uses flex-wrap for responsive behavior
justify-content and align-items are used for alignment
A card or grid-like pattern is created with consistent spacing

lessons.title: Variables & Data Types
objectives: Declare variables with let and const and understand when to use each
Work with strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, and objects
Use template literals to build dynamic strings
hints: Use const for values that never change (like a tax rate), let for values that do (like a running total)
Template literals use backticks and ${} — `Hello, ${name}!` inserts the variable's value
Arrays hold ordered lists: ['apple', 'banana']. Objects hold named properties: { name: 'Ada', age: 30 }
assessmentCriteria: Variables are declared with const and let appropriately (no var)
At least 3 different data types are used
Template literals are used for string building
An array and an object are created with real-world data

lessons.title: Functions That Do Real Work
objectives: Write functions with parameters, default values, and return statements
Use arrow functions and understand when they're appropriate
Build utility functions that solve practical problems
hints: A function should do one thing well — if you're writing 'and' in the description, consider splitting it
Arrow functions: const add = (a, b) => a + b; — great for short, simple operations
Default parameters: function greet(name = 'friend') lets you call greet() without arguments
assessmentCriteria: At least one function declaration and one arrow function are used
Functions use parameters with at least one default value
All functions return values (no relying on side effects alone)
Functions solve real calculation or formatting problems
